title: Rename variables in sorting engines for self-documenting readability
description: I3 audit: sorting engines use competitive-programmer variable names (a, i, j, n, temp) that obscure meaning for learning. Rename in all 6 runnable sorting engines: a→array or items, i→passIndex, j→compareIndex, n→arrayLength, temp→swapTemp. Also rename in step descriptions for consistency. Graph engines (bfs.ts, dijkstra.ts) already use better names (color, dist, queue) — sorting should match that standard. Keep pseudocode unchanged (pseudocode traditionally uses short names).
acceptanceCriteria: bubble-sort.ts: a→array, n→length, i→pass, j→comparePos
quick-sort.ts: a→array, pivot→pivotValue, i→partitionBoundary
merge-sort.ts: a→array, leftArr→leftHalf, rightArr→rightHalf
Step descriptions updated to use new names
Pseudocode remains unchanged (traditional notation)
All tests still pass after rename

title: Add Hoare partition variant for Quick Sort alongside existing Lomuto
description: I8 audit: every algorithm has exactly ONE variant. Quick Sort is the most important to show variants for — Lomuto (current, simpler) vs Hoare (faster, used in production). Add a variant selector: 'Partition Scheme: Lomuto | Hoare'. Hoare implementation processes from both ends toward middle. Step descriptions should highlight the difference: 'Hoare uses two pointers converging from both ends — fewer swaps than Lomuto but harder to implement correctly.'
acceptanceCriteria: Variant selector visible when Quick Sort is selected
Hoare partition produces correct sorted output
Step descriptions explain the two-pointer convergence approach
Comparison note: 'Lomuto: ~n/2 swaps avg. Hoare: ~n/3 swaps avg. Hoare is faster but harder to implement.'
Both variants share the same config/metadata but different engines

title: Add iterative DFS variant alongside existing recursive DFS
description: I8 audit: DFS only has recursive implementation. Iterative DFS using an explicit stack shows students HOW the call stack works — making the implicit explicit. Add variant selector: 'Style: Recursive | Iterative'. Iterative version uses a stack array with push/pop. Step descriptions should highlight: 'Notice the stack mirrors what the recursive version does with the call stack. Each pop is like a function return.'
acceptanceCriteria: Variant selector visible when DFS is selected
Iterative DFS produces same traversal order as recursive
Stack visualization shown alongside graph (or stack contents in step description)
Step descriptions explain: 'Pop node X from stack (equivalent to recursive DFS returning from X)'
Comparison note at end: 'Both visit the same nodes in the same order. Iterative avoids stack overflow on deep graphs.'

title: Add bottom-up Merge Sort variant alongside existing top-down
description: I8 audit: Merge Sort only has top-down recursive variant. Bottom-up (iterative) Merge Sort avoids recursion entirely — important for students who struggle with recursion. Add variant selector. Bottom-up starts with pairs, merges into groups of 4, then 8, etc. Step descriptions: 'No recursion here — we start with the smallest pieces and build up. This is the bottom-up approach.'
acceptanceCriteria: Variant selector: 'Style: Top-Down (Recursive) | Bottom-Up (Iterative)'
Bottom-up produces same sorted output
Step descriptions explain the iterative merging strategy
Comparison: 'Same O(n log n) performance, but bottom-up uses O(1) extra space for recursion (only O(n) for merge buffer).'

title: Add educational inline comments to top 10 algorithm engine files
description: I10 audit: most comments restate code ('// swap elements') or are absent. Educational comments explain WHY: '// Lomuto invariant: everything in arr[low..i] is ≤ pivot. This makes correctness easy to prove.' Add 5-10 educational comments per engine file for top 10 algorithms. Comments should form a NARRATIVE — reading just the comments should teach the algorithm. Reference: Sedgewick's 'Algorithms' textbook uses this approach.
acceptanceCriteria: Each of top 10 engine files has 5+ educational comments
Comments explain WHY, not WHAT (no '// swap elements')
Key decision points have comments: why this pivot? why this data structure? why this order?
Comments reference the algorithm's invariant where applicable
Reading ONLY the comments gives a coherent explanation of the algorithm

title: Add Python display code for top 10 algorithms (matching TypeScript implementation)
description: I7 audit: zero display code in any language. Students want to see algorithms in THEIR language. Add a 'View Code' panel that shows clean, readable Python implementation for each algorithm. Python chosen because: (1) most CS courses use it, (2) most readable for beginners, (3) LeetCode/NeetCode solutions are mostly Python. Code should match the TypeScript implementation's logic exactly. Display with syntax highlighting in a togglable panel.
acceptanceCriteria: View Code button visible in properties panel for top 10 algorithms
Python code displayed with syntax highlighting
Code logic matches the TypeScript engine exactly (same variant, same steps)
Code is clean, PEP 8 compliant, with educational comments
Copy-to-clipboard button for code

title: Add 'Textbook vs Production' comparison for top 5 algorithms
description: I9 audit: zero connection to real-world implementations. Add a comparison panel showing: our teaching version vs the REAL production version. For Merge Sort: our clean recursive version vs Python's Timsort (hybrid with insertion sort for small runs). For Dijkstra: our priority queue version vs Google Maps' contraction hierarchies. For Quick Sort: our Lomuto vs C stdlib's introsort (quicksort + heapsort fallback). Show the GAP between textbook and production, and explain WHY production versions are different.
acceptanceCriteria: Comparison accessible from a 'Real-World' tab in properties panel
For 5 algorithms: shows 'Textbook Version' vs 'Production Version'
Each comparison explains WHY the production version differs
Merge Sort: mentions Timsort (Python/Java), explains hybrid approach
Dijkstra: mentions contraction hierarchies (Google Maps), explains preprocessing
Quick Sort: mentions introsort (C++), explains fallback to heapsort

title: Fix DP table cumulative state — previously computed cells should stay colored
description: V1 audit: DPVisualizer.tsx:90-111 computes cell states from ONLY the current step's mutations. Previously computed cells revert to 'default' between steps. A cell that was 'computed' (gray-600) should STAY 'computed' in subsequent steps, not disappear. The visualizer needs to maintain cumulative state across all steps up to the current one, not just the current step's mutations. [NEEDS-RUNTIME-VERIFICATION — observed from code analysis of DPVisualizer useMemo that only reads step.mutations]
acceptanceCriteria: DP cells marked 'computed' in step N remain visually 'computed' in steps N+1, N+2, ...
Only the current 'computing' cell pulses/scales
The 'optimal' path cells highlighted during backtracking stay highlighted
Cumulative state tracked either in visualizer or via accumulated mutations from PlaybackController

title: Fix post-mutation step descriptions in bubble-sort, quick-sort, and heap-sort
description: P0 VISUAL LIE found by implementation audit agent: In 3 sorting engines, step descriptions are generated AFTER the in-place swap, causing displayed values to be post-swap (inverted). bubble-sort.ts:86-88 swaps, then line 126 reads a[j] and a[j+1] which now hold swapped values. Same pattern in quick-sort.ts:130-132→138 and heap-sort.ts:160-162→168. Fix: capture const preSwapLeft = a[j], preSwapRight = a[j+1] BEFORE the swap, use those in description strings. Merge Sort does NOT have this bug because it uses immutable snapshots (leftArr, rightArr) from a.slice().
acceptanceCriteria: bubble-sort.ts: swap description shows pre-swap values, not post-swap
quick-sort.ts: partition swap description shows pre-swap values
heap-sort.ts: heapify swap and extract swap descriptions show pre-swap values
Verified by tracing with [5,3,8,1,9]: every step description matches the visual state BEFORE the operation
Unit test added: verify step description values match pre-mutation array state

title: Fix Dijkstra complexity claim OR replace array-sort with real min-heap
description: P0 found by implementation audit: dijkstra.ts CONFIG claims O((V+E) log V) time complexity but the actual implementation uses pq.sort() per iteration (line 112) giving O(V² log V). This is TEACHING WRONG COMPLEXITY to students. Either: (A) Replace pq.sort()+shift() with a proper binary min-heap to match the O((V+E) log V) claim, OR (B) Change CONFIG to O(V²) and add note 'This simplified version uses array scanning. Production versions use a binary heap for O((V+E) log V).' Option A is better for teaching — students should see the algorithm they'll use in interviews.
acceptanceCriteria: Dijkstra's time complexity claim in AlgorithmConfig matches the actual implementation
If option A: min-heap implemented with O(log V) extract-min and decrease-key
If option B: complexity changed to O(V² + E) with explanation in description
Step descriptions mention the priority queue operation being performed
Existing tests still pass
